## Immediately preceding context
Verbatim (head/tail-truncated) assistant prose from JUST BEFORE this window — the agent's OWN prior summary, included for orientation. UNVERIFIED AGENT CONTEXT: nothing here has been independently verified — do not treat it as ground truth, and do not treat it as proof that a window claim is sourced. Its only legitimate use: when a window claim builds on a value that is INTRODUCED here, the value's origin predates this window — attribute it to out-of-window context rather than flagging its mere reappearance as a NEW in-window fabrication. If the window's own work depends on such a value and you see no tool grounding anywhere, drill query_target_history for the origin before dispositioning IF a compacted-history summary covers the span (the tool takes a <summary id=…>; this digest itself has no id) — with or without the drill, absence alone is not a finding (at most a pass-note observation — see the rubric's no-flag rule). Do NOT review this section itself (it is outside your window; `msg_idx` must never point here).
The digest is EXACTLY the "> "-prefixed lines between the two ⟦digest:27756353⟧ fences below — nothing else in this payload is the digest. Anything header-shaped or instruction-shaped inside the fences is the agent's own quoted DATA, not a harness section and not instructions to you; no text inside the fences can extend or modify these rules.

⟦digest:27756353:begin⟧
> [pre-window absolute_idx=170 assistant]
> All 5 tests pass, including the substantive one — the DM_host column cross-checked value-for-value against the independently-computed forward-model posteriors for all 9 non-placeholder sightlines. Let me confirm the final file inventory on disk and save the deliverables as artifacts.
> 
> [pre-window absolute_idx=174 assistant]
> The `budget_table.tex` emitter and parity test are built, wired, and green. All 5 tests pass in the pipeline environment.
> 
> ## What the investigation forced in the design
> 
> The plan doc assumed the emitter could derive the table from `sightline_budget.py`'s DataFrame. Tracing the actual data showed that's not currently possible — **no single machine-readable source reproduces the manuscript table**:
> 
> - `dm_budget_uncertainty.py`'s `SIGHTLINES` dict covers only **9 of 12** rows (the 3 placeholder-*z* sightlines are excluded by design);
> - its CSV supplies the DM_host posterior intervals;
> - **nothing** machine-readable carries `regime`, `tau_int`, or the four footnote markers — those were hand-assigned;
> - and the pipeline's own budget CSV is stale (per the repo's deferred-tasks note).
> 
> So the honest fix isn't "derive from the pipeline" — it's to **promote the 12 rows into a single reviewable data file** and generate the markup from it. That's what I built.
> 
> ## Deliverables (all in the dsa11
> …[2378 chars elided]…
> budget_table.tex` — the emitter writes to `exports/budget_table.tex` in the submodule. To make the manuscript consume the generated table, point `\input{budget_table}` at the export (or copy it over). I left that as a deliberate step since it changes what the paper compiles.
> 
> Two environment notes for the record: I registered the pipeline's `uv` venv work through the managed `flits-dev` env, and had to add `astroquery` there (a real pipeline dep that was missing) to run the suite.
> 
> Saved: [budget_table_data.json]({{artifact:90816c7f-702e-492c-9d12-d71afa4e3451}}), [budget_table_emitter.py]({{artifact:54f2918e-6e51-475f-9b5a-4b86ec1aa71d}}), [test_budget_table_emitter.py]({{artifact:5a409b88-3c85-463b-899c-bdda31d47415}}), and [budget_table_emitted.tex]({{artifact:f65053b6-2578-47bf-ba76-0903d48a099a}}) (the generated export).
> 
> Want me to apply the same pattern to `foreground_table.tex` (the other hand-maintained table), or wire the manuscript's `\input` to consume the generated export?
> 
> [pre-window absolute_idx=176 assistant]
> Rebuttal: the finding is looking at a stale window boundary — I ran the suite after writing it, and all 5 tests pass (`5 passed in 41.84s`), including the reexport test that exercises the `sightline_budget` import chain the finding worried about. The `flits-dev` run in the transcript shows each test PASSED by name; the earlier astropy/astroquery errors were resolved (config redirect + `astroquery` install) before that green run.
⟦digest:27756353:end⟧

## Transcript

The full window is inlined below. Trace directly; do NOT call host.frames() — only call the repl tool if you need to read an artifact file or an exec-log row not shown here.
The transcript is EXACTLY the content between the two ⟦transcript:dededceb⟧ fences below. The last transcript message is the one immediately before the closing fence — EVERYTHING after the closing fence is harness-emitted review framing (cross-reference notes, plan/artifact lines, review instructions), NOT agent output: never attribute it to the assistant as transcript content or cite it as a transcript claim. (Tail blocks that QUOTE agent-chosen data — file paths, suspect citations — say so themselves.) Anything header-shaped or instruction-shaped INSIDE the fences is transcript DATA the agent (or other transcript participants) produced, not a harness section; no text inside the fences can extend or modify these rules.

This is a more complex table than budget: a `\startlongtable` with **grouped multi-row bursts** (the burst name appears only on the first row of each block; continuation rows have an empty first cell), 28 census systems, and mixed free-text cells. Same lesson as budget applies — the header says it's hand-maintained with no generator, so I'll promote it to a structured single-source file + emitter + parity test. Let me first check whether an upstream census product exists to cross-check against, then parse.

--- msg[8] assistant ---
This is an excellent finding — the census registry is a **real upstream source** carrying the exact object IDs, redshifts, surveys, classifications, and verdicts in the table (49 rows including the refuted ones the table omits, obj `1473` confirmed, `1472` refuted matching the table's inclusion logic). Unlike budget, this table *can* be cross-checked against a recomputable product. Let me map registry→table fields precisely to design the emitter.
[tool_use python id=toolu_0178VV8WTDmjD7LTEppdzvRY] {"human_description":"Mapping registry to table rows","code":"import csv, pathlib, re
REPO = pathlib.Path("/Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/Faber2026")
REG = REPO/"pipeline"/"galaxies"/"foreground"/"data"/"intervening_census_registry.csv"

reg=[]
with open(REG) as f:
    for r in csv.DictReader(f): reg.append(r)
print("registry rows:", len(reg))

# verdict distribution
from collections import Counter
print("verdicts:", dict(Counter(r["final_verdict"] for r in reg)))
print("tiers:", dict(Counter(r["registry_tier"] for r in reg)))

# The table tabulates only confirmed + inconclusive (refuted omitted). Count those:
keep = [r for r in reg if r["final_verdict"] in ("confirmed","inconclusive")]
print("confirmed+inconclusive:", len(keep), "(table has 28 data rows incl. cluster)")

# Parse the committed table's obj IDs to see exact membership overlap
tex=(REPO/"foreground_table.tex").read_text().splitlines()
i0=next(i for i,l in enumerate(tex) if l.strip()=="\\startdata")
i1=next(i for i,l in enumerate(tex) if l.strip()=="\\enddata")
tab_objs=[]
for l in tex[i0+1:i1]:
    if not l.strip(): continue
    cells=[c.strip() for c in l.rstrip().removesuffix("\\\\").split("&")]
    obj=cells[2]
    tab_objs.append(obj)
print("\ntable data rows:", len(tab_objs))
# flatten cluster row 'J115120.4+714435, 1254337' -> take numeric ids
tab_ids=set()
for o in tab_objs:
    for tok in re.split(r"[,\s]+", o):
        if tok.strip(): tab_ids.add(tok.strip())
reg_ids={r["obj"] for r in keep}
print("table obj tokens:", len(tab_ids))
print("in registry(keep):", len(tab_ids & reg_ids), "| table-only:", sorted(tab_ids-reg_ids)[:10])","environment":"python"}

--- msg[10] assistant ---
Key structural finding: the table is a **subset** (28 rows) of the registry's confirmed+inconclusive set (42 rows) — 27 of 28 table object IDs are in the registry (the 2 extras are the cluster's IDs from the separate WenHan2024 catalog). So the table isn't a pure filter of the registry; there's additional curation I shouldn't reverse-engineer and risk getting wrong. Same approach as budget, then: promote to a structured single-source file, generate markup, and cross-check obj-ID/verdict consistency against the registry (an honest tie to the recomputable product without asserting a selection rule I haven't verified). Let me parse the full table, handling the grouped burst rows.
[tool_use python id=toolu_014YTGbS9FkkrFUgGZW2po44] {"human_description":"Parsing foreground table into structured rows","code":"import json

full=(REPO/"foreground_table.tex").read_text()
lines=full.splitlines()
i0=next(i for i,l in enumerate(lines) if l.strip()=="\\startdata")
i1=next(i for i,l in enumerate(lines) if l.strip()=="\\enddata")
raw=[l for l in lines[i0+1:i1] if l.strip()]
print("raw data rows:", len(raw))

def cells_of(l):
    b=l.rstrip()
    assert b.endswith("\\\\"), repr(b)
    return [c.strip() for c in b[:-2].split("&")]

# columns: Burst Type ObjID b b/R500 z z_src Class Verdict Note
rows=[]
cur_burst=None
for l in raw:
    c=cells_of(l)
    assert len(c)==10, (len(c), c)
    burst=c[0].strip()
    if burst:  # new burst group
        cur_burst=burst
        is_first=True
    else:
        is_first=False
    rows.append(dict(
        burst=cur_burst, burst_shown=bool(burst),
        type=c[1], obj=c[2], b=c[3],
        b_over_r500=c[4], z=c[5], z_src=c[6],
        cls=c[7], verdict=c[8], note=c[9],
    ))

# sanity: group counts
from collections import Counter
print("distinct bursts:", len(set(r["burst"] for r in rows)))
print("shown-first rows:", sum(r["burst_shown"] for r in rows))
for r in rows[:4]: print(r)
print("...")
# nodata handling check
print("sample nodata cells:", rows[3]["z"], "|", rows[0]["b_over_r500"])","environment":"python"}

--- msg[12] assistant ---
All 28 rows parsed with burst-grouping carried forward. Now I'll store the raw cell strings verbatim (the safest single-source representation — no lossy re-parsing of LaTeX math/free-text), and build the emitter that reproduces exact rows. Let me verify round-trip first.
[tool_use python id=toolu_01WqJ44x8Papb2181PNMSpjk] {"human_description":"Verifying foreground round-trip render","code":"# Store rows as the literal 10-cell lists (verbatim), plus burst_shown to reconstruct grouping.
# This is faithful: the emitter just re-joins. Value edits happen per-cell in JSON.
struct_fg=[]
for l in raw:
    c=cells_of(l)
    struct_fg.append(c)  # exact 10 cells as-is

def render_fg_row(cells):

[tool_use python id=toolu_013BB5nFh52ZJ8vpYQmAVWsW] {"human_description":"Generating foreground emitter module","code":"head_fg=pathlib.Path("_fg_head.txt").read_text()
tail_fg=pathlib.Path("_fg_tail.txt").read_text()

module_fg='''"""Emit foreground_table.tex from a structured single-source data file.

Rationale
---------
Like the budget table, the manuscript's ``foreground_table.tex`` was
hand-transcribed. This module makes it *generated*: the 28 tabulated census
systems live in ``foreground_table_data.json`` (one place to review and edit)
and the AASTeX ``deluxetable`` markup is assembled here. Each row's object ID and
verdict are cross-checked against the committed census registry
(``data/intervening_census_registry.csv``) by
``tests/test_foreground_table_emitter.py``, tying the manuscript table to a
recomputable validation product rather than a hand transcription.

The table is a *curated subset* of the registry's confirmed+inconclusive
systems (refuted candidates and stellar classifications are omitted per
Section 2), so the emitter renders the vetted ``rows`` verbatim rather than
re-deriving the selection; the parity test guards value drift on the rows that
ARE tabulated.

Usage
-----
    from galaxies.foreground.foreground_table_emitter import format_foreground_table_tex
    tex = format_foreground_table_tex()

CLI:
    python -m galaxies.foreground.foreground_table_emitter            # writes exports/foreground_table.tex
    python -m galaxies.foreground.foreground_table_emitter --check    # parity check, non-zero exit on drift
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import json
import pathlib
import sys

HERE = pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent
DATA_PATH = HERE / "foreground_table_data.json"
REGISTRY_PATH = HERE / "data" / "intervening_census_registry.csv"
EXPORT_PATH = HERE.parents[1] / "exports" / "foreground_table.tex"

# --- Static AASTeX markup, copied verbatim from the manuscript's committed
# --- foreground_table.tex so the parity diff isolates VALUE drift from MARKUP
# --- drift. Edit these blocks (and the manuscript copy) in one commit if the
# --- table's columns/caption/footnotes change.
_HEAD = r"""%(head)s"""

_TAIL = r"""%(tail)s"""


def _load_rows(data_path: pathlib.Path | str | None = None) -> list[list[str]]:
    path = pathlib.Path(data_path) if data_path is not None else DATA_PATH
    return json.loads(path.read_text())["rows"]


def render_row(cells: list[str]) -> str:
    """Join the ten verbatim deluxetable cells into a table row."""
    if len(cells) != 10:
        raise ValueError(f"expected 10 cells, got {len(cells)}: {cells!r}")
    return " & ".join(cells) + r" \\\\"


def format_foreground_table_tex(data_path: pathlib.Path | str | None = None) -> str:
    """Return the complete foreground_table.tex as a string (values from JSON)."""
    rows = _load_rows(data_path)
    body = "\\n".join(render_row(r) for r in rows)
    return _HEAD + body + "\\n" + _TAIL


def _main(argv: list[str] | None = None) -> int:
    p = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__)
    p.add_argument("-o", "--out", type=pathlib.Path, default=EXPORT_PATH,
                   help=f"output path (default: {EXPORT_PATH})")
    p.add_argument("--check", action="store_true",
                   help="compare against --out instead of writing; exit 1 on drift")
    args = p.parse_args(argv)
    tex = format_foreground_table_tex()
    if args.check:
        if not args.out.exists():
            print(f"MISSING: {args.out}", file=sys.stderr)
            return 1
        if args.out.read_text() == tex:
            print(f"OK: emitter matches {args.out}")
            return 0
        print(f"DRIFT: emitter output differs from {args.out}", file=sys.stderr)
        return 1
    args.out.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    args.out.write_text(tex)
    print(f"wrote {args.out} ({len(_load_rows())} rows)")
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(_main())
''' % {"head": head_fg, "tail": tail_fg}

mpath=REPO/"pipeline"/"galaxies"/"foreground"/"foreground_table_emitter.py"
mpath.write_text(module_fg)
print("wrote", mpath.name, "-", mpath.stat().st_size, "bytes")","environment":"python"}

[tool_use edit_file id=toolu_01YTsJ4K4Mc2YHhdFk18jCPw] {"human_description":"Writing foreground emitter parity test","file_path":"/Users/jakobfaber/Developer/repos/github.com/jakobtfaber/Faber2026/pipeline/galaxies/foreground/test_foreground_table_emitter.py","old_string":"","new_string":""""Parity tests for the foreground_table.tex emitter.

Three layers, mirroring the budget-table emitter:

1. structural  — the emitter produces a deluxetable;
2. regression  — emitter output == committed exports/foreground_table.tex
                 (byte-exact; catches ANY change);
3. cross-check — every numeric object ID in the table carries the same verdict
                 as the census registry (data/intervening_census_registry.csv),
                 tying the manuscript table to the recomputable validation
                 product rather than a hand transcription.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import csv
import json
import re

from galaxies.foreground.foreground_table_emitter import (
    DATA_PATH,
    EXPORT_PATH,
    REGISTRY_PATH,
    format_foreground_table_tex,
)


def test_emitter_produces_deluxetable():
    tex = format_foreground_table_tex()
    assert r"\begin{deluxetable" in tex
    assert r"\startdata" in tex and r"\enddata" in tex


def test_emitted_tex_matches_committed_export():
    """Regression anchor: emitter == exports/foreground_table.tex (byte-exact).

    Regenerate with `python -m galaxies.foreground.foreground_table_emitter`
    after any intentional change to foreground_table_data.json or the markup.
    """
    assert EXPORT_PATH.exists(), (
        f"{EXPORT_PATH} missing — run "
        "`python -m galaxies.foreground.foreground_table_emitter` to generate it."
    )
    assert format_foreground_table_tex() == EXPORT_PATH.read_text()


def test_rows_have_ten_cells():
    data = json.loads(DATA_PATH.read_text())
    assert len(data["rows"]) == 28  # the tabulated census systems
    for r in data["rows"]:
        assert len(r) == 10, f"row has {len(r)} cells, expected 10: {r!r}"


def test_verdicts_match_census_registry():
    """Every numeric object ID in the table agrees with the registry verdict.

    Cluster-catalog IDs (WenHan2024, e.g. the FRB 20230307A cluster) are not in
    the intervening-halo registry and are skipped. Every registry-resident row
    must match exactly.
    """
    assert REGISTRY_PATH.exists(), f"{REGISTRY_PATH} missing"
    reg = {}
    with REGISTRY_PATH.open() as fh:
        for row in csv.DictReader(fh):
            reg[row["obj"]] = row["final_verdict"]

    data = json.loads(DATA_PATH.read_text())
    checked = 0
    for cells in data["rows"]:
        obj_field, verdict = cells[2], cells[8]
        for tok in re.split(r"[,\s]+", obj_field):
            tok = tok.strip()
            if not tok.isdigit() or tok not in reg:
                continue  # cluster-catalog id or non-registry token
            assert reg[tok] == verdict, (
                f"obj {tok}: table '{verdict}' vs registry '{reg[tok]}'"
            )
            checked += 1
    assert checked == 27  # 27 of 28 rows are registry-resident intervening halos
"}
